summary: The notebook implements an autonomous coding agent pipeline designed for the Konwinski Prize competition. It provisions a local vLLM server running Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct and an Elasticsearch instance to index Python codebases. Using LangGraph, it constructs a stateful agent workflow equipped with tools for file operations and semantic code search. The agent receives a GitHub issue and a repository archive, analyzes the code structure, and generates a git diff patch to fix the reported bug. Readers learn how to integrate LLMs with external tools, search databases, and code parsing libraries to build autonomous software engineering agents.
competition_flow: Unpacks a repository archive, parses its Python files into a flattened syntax tree, indexes the structure in Elasticsearch, runs a LangGraph agent powered by a locally served Qwen2.5-14B model to analyze and edit the code, and outputs a git diff patch for submission.
data_reading: Unpacks a `.tar` archive from a `BytesIO` buffer into a local `repo` directory.
data_processing: Parses Python source files using `tree-sitter` to extract hierarchical syntax trees, flattens them into a list of dictionaries containing node types, file paths, text snippets, and line/column coordinates, then indexes this structured data into Elasticsearch.
features_engineering: Flattened syntax tree nodes (function/class definitions) with parent-child relationships, file paths, and start/end line/column coordinates indexed for BM25 search.
insights: Integrating a local LLM with Elasticsearch enables efficient, context-aware codebase navigation for autonomous agents.
Flattening hierarchical AST nodes into a searchable index preserves structural relationships while enabling fast BM25 retrieval.
LangGraph's stateful workflow effectively manages the iterative tool-calling loop required for complex code editing tasks.
